coredata.dc:title: Corncob residues as carbon quantum dots sources and their application in detection of metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Liguo
Wang, Yu
Liu, Wei
Ni, Yonghao
Hou, Qingxi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been attracting extensive attention due to their chemical inertness, high water solubility, size effect, non-toxicity, photoluminescence reaction, photo-induced electron transfer, and tunable light emissions, among other attributes. In the study, the CQDs were synthesized using a simple, green, energy-saving, and efficient hydrothermal method from corncob residues (CRs) obtained after hemicelluloses pre-extraction of corncobs, and the enzymatically hydrolyzed corncob residues (EHCRs). The resultant CQDs were characterized and their applications in detection of metal ions were investigated. The results showed that CRs and EHCRs were good sources for CQDs; the nitrogen doping improved the fluorescence intensity and stability of CQDs; the nitrogen-doped CQDs from EHCRs (E-N-CQDs) exhibited the strongest fluorescence compared with other CQDs; the E-N-CQDs had a strong emission at a wavelength of 414 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength of 318 nm, the strongest fluorescence intensity was found at pH 10.0; the E-N-CQDs were the most sensitive to Fe(III) with the detection limit of 0.75 μmol L−1, which was much lower than the concentration of Fe(III) in human blood (7.52 − 11.82 mmol L−1). The developed E-N-CQDs concept may provide a novel method for the detection of Fe(III) in human blood.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corncob residues
Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen
Fluorescence
Fe(III)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of E-N-CQDs for detection of metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of route and synthesis of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs for sensing Fe(III).
FTIR spectra of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs.

UV–vis absorption spectra and photographs of E-N-CQDs (left) and N-CQDs (right) excited by a 365 nm UV lamp (a); photoluminescent EX and EM spectra of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs (b); and 3D fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs (c) and E-N-CQDs (d).
Morphologies of the E-N-CQDs from TEM (a), HRTEM (b), and AFM (c, d).

XPS analysis of E-N-CQDs (a), high resolution C1s core level XPS spectrum for EHCRs (b); high resolution C1s (c), O1s (d), and N1s (e) core levels of E-N-CQDs; SEM and mapping images of C,O and N elements (f) of E-N-CQDs.
Effect of pH on photoluminescent of E-N-CQDs.

F/F0 of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs after adding different metal ions (10 mmol L−1) (a); fluorescence intensities of E-N-CQDs after adding different dosages of Fe(III) ions (b), in which the inset is the F0 /F of E-N-CQDs under different concentrations of Fe(III).
Content of elements on the surface of CRs, N-CQDs, EHCRs, and E-N-CQDs (in atomic % from XPS analysis).
Results of deconvolution of C1s, O1s and N2p core energy levels from XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Corncob residues as carbon quantum dots sources and their application in detection of metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredercton, New Brunswick, E3B 5A3, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been attracting extensive attention due to their chemical inertness, high water solubility, size effect, non-toxicity, photoluminescence reaction, photo-induced electron transfer, and tunable light emissions, among other attributes. In the study, the CQDs were synthesized using a simple, green, energy-saving, and efficient hydrothermal method from corncob residues (CRs) obtained after hemicelluloses pre-extraction of corncobs, and the enzymatically hydrolyzed corncob residues (EHCRs). The resultant CQDs were characterized and their applications in detection of metal ions were investigated. The results showed that CRs and EHCRs were good sources for CQDs; the nitrogen doping improved the fluorescence intensity and stability of CQDs; the nitrogen-doped CQDs from EHCRs (E-N-CQDs) exhibited the strongest fluorescence compared with other CQDs; the E-N-CQDs had a strong emission at a wavelength of 414 nm and an optimal excitation wavelength of 318 nm, the strongest fluorescence intensity was found at pH 10.0; the E-N-CQDs were the most sensitive to Fe(III) with the detection limit of 0.75 μmol L−1, which was much lower than the concentration of Fe(III) in human blood (7.52 − 11.82 mmol L−1). The developed E-N-CQDs concept may provide a novel method for the detection of Fe(III) in human blood.

ce:section.ce:para: The agricultural and forestry residues, by-products generated in agricultural and forestry production and processing, and residues from food processing industry, have been main part of lignocellulosic biomass. With the increase of agricultural residues in the process of production and operation, and the loss of rural labor force, the utilization rate of agricultural residues has been significantly reduced. In addition, most of the agricultural and forestry residues are burned, causing serious environmental pollution. As one of main grain crops in China, corn yield ranks third among the crop products. In 2017, corn production was about 2.16 × 108 t, about 16%−18% of which was corncob, so about 3.5 × 107−3.9 × 107 t corncob can be obtained. Many enterprises have taken corncob as the raw materials for extracting hemicelluloses to produce xylitol, furfural, ethanol, and so on (Deng et al., 2016; Di et al., 2014; Mao et al., 2013). It provides a good utilization of corncob resources, and also contributes to the increase of farmers' income, which brings certain social and economic benefits. However, it also leads to a large amount of corncob residues (CRs). The CRs are usually considered as solid wastes to burn directly, which not only generates low energy recovery rate but also results in a series of environmental problems. At present, many researches have been focusing on the utilization of corncob and its residues (Wang et al., 2016). The utilization of corncob and its residues changes the present situation, and creates more opportunities for sustainable and green development in society and the economy.

In the past decade, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become a new generation of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials due to their excellent optical properties, non-toxicity, high water-solubility, biocompatibility, and so on (Atchudan et al., 2018; Farshbaf et al., 2018; Fernando et al., 2015). CQDs were discovered when single-walled carbon nanotubes were separated and purified by Xu et al (Xu et al., 2004). Sun et al. synthesized a fluorescent nanoparticle with a reinforcing effect fluorescence emission through the surface passivation, and named it “carbon quantum dot” (Sun et al., 2006). At present, there are two routes to synthesis CQDs, i.e., (1) the top-down route including electrochemical oxidation (Wang et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2008), acidic oxidation, arc discharge, and laser ablation (Cao et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2010); (2) the bottom-up route such as hydrothermal and microwave method (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013; Zhuo et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). Introducing appropriate functional groups for surface passivation can improve the fluorescence intensity of CQDs (Atchudan et al., 2018). In recent years, it was found that doping nitrogen in CQDs can improve the graphene structure and bandwidth-energy gap within CQDs, thus improving the fluorescence quantum yield, optical properties, reactivity and catalytic performance of CQDs (Yao et al., 2015). Citrate and ammonia based compounds have been commonly used as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to prepare nitrogen-doped carbons quantum dots (N-CQDs). In addition, some other materials such as glucose (Chen et al., 2018), maltol & sucrose (Tang et al., 2017), and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (Ding et al., 2014) have also been reported as raw materials for synthesizing CQDs. However, these raw materials are much more expensive than agricultural and forestry residues, and will bring some environment issues during the production. In our previous work, we found that the weight percentage of cellulose and lignin in corncob residues was as high as 50 − 60% and 20 − 30%, respectively. Cellulose, a polysaccharide consisting of a linear chain of β(1→4) linked D-glucose units, is the most abundant organic compound in nature. Lignin is formed by aryl ether and CC bonds connecting guaiacyl (G), sinapyl (S), and p-hydroxyphenyl (H), all of which are aromatic compounds (Wang et al., 2017). Utilization of carbon-rich agricultural residues as raw materials will further promote the application of N-CQDs in various aspects.

In this study, two kinds of CQDs were successfully synthesized by one-pot reaction from the CRs obtained after hemicelluloses pre-extraction and their enzymatically hydrolyzed corncob residues (EHCRs). The main components of CRs are cellulose and lignin, while the main component of EHCRs is lignin. The hypothesis is that the fluorescence performance of CQDs is related to the components of raw materials, a large number of aromatic rings in lignin can further improve the fluorescence performance. The properties of as-prepared N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs were evaluated and compared by using FTIR, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atom force microscopy (AFM) were used to further characterize the properties of E-N-CQDs. E-N-CQDs were then applied as fluorescent probes for detecting metal ions, especially Fe(III). The route and fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs by Fe(III) is illustrated in Scheme 1 . To our knowledge, it is the first time that corncob residues were used to produce CQDs, and to explore their potential application in detecting Fe(III). The conversion of such residues into photoluminiscent materials will have great potential applications in medicine, sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and other fields.

The CRs were collected from a factory in Henan province, China. The corncobs were pretreated with dilute sulfuric acid to prepare xylitol in the factory. Prior to the experiment, the CRs were air dried, mechanically pulverized, and sieved with a 40–60 mesh screen.

The chemical compositions of the CRs were measured at 50.31% glucan, 3.27% xylan, 27.01% acid-insoluble lignin, 3.06% acid-soluble lignin, 12.10% ethanol extractives, and 3.06% ash (based on oven-dry weight of CRs), which were determined according to NREL/TP-510-42618 and NREL/TP-510-42619.

The CRs were treated by a basket mill (RT-LMO.75, Shanghai Root Mechanical and Electrical Equipment Co., Ltd., China) at a solid to liquid ratio of 1:25 (w/v) at 1000 rpm for 45 min. The ground CRs were regarded as the substrates for the following enzymatic hydrolysis. The enzymatic hydrolysis was performed in a 100 mL conical flask using sodium acetate as a buffer (50 mmol L−1, pH 4.8) at 5.0% (w/w) solid contents. The enzymatic hydrolysis was conducted at 50 °C in an incubator (IKA KS 4000i control, IKA, Germany) at 180 rpm for up to 96 h. The cellulase was added at a loading of 33 FPU/g substrates to remove most of cellulose in CRs. Then, the enzymatically hydrolyzed corncob residues (EHCRs) were collected for further use. The main chemical compositions of the EHCRs were: 34.60% glucan, 3.48% xylan, 46.05% acid-insoluble lignin, 2.26% acid-soluble lignin, 10.02% ethanol extractives, and 2.85% ash (based on oven-dry weight of EHCRs), which was measured according to the NREL standards NREL/TP-510-42618 and NREL/TP-510-42619.

The enzymes with activity of 138 FPU/mL were provided by Vlan Biotech Group (Qingdao, China). D-(+)-glucose was used as the standard and was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. CuSO4, Al(NO3)3, AgNO3, and CdCl2 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, China. ZnCl2, Ni(CH3COO)2, Fe2(SO4)3, and KCl were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. All of the chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade.

6.0 g CRs and 6 mL ammonium water were dispersed in 54 mL of deionized water, mixed uniformly and kept stirring for 5 min. The solution was then added into a stainless steel autoclave (4566, Parr Instrument Company, USA), and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction completed, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained mixture was filtered using a 0.22 μm cylindrical filtration membrane filter to remove the aggregates of large particles. The solution was dialyzed in deionized water using a semi-permeable membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 D for 2 days. During this process, deionized water was changed every 6 h. The final solution was subjected to lyophilization to obtain the N-CQDs.
The nitrogen-doped CQDs generated from EHCRs were obtained in the same way, i.e., E-N-CQDs.

CQDs were characterized using various analytical techniques. The FTIR spectra were obtained on a spectrophotometer (650, Tianjin Gangdong Sci. &Tech. Development Co, Ltd., China), with the KBr pellet method on the powdered samples. The spectra were recorded in the range of 4000 − 400 cm−1, and scanned 32 times at the speed of 4°/min. UV–vis absorption spectrum of test sample was determined by a spectrophotometer (DR-6000, HACH, USA). Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared samples were measured with a HITACHI F-7100 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Ltd., Japan). TEM and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the E-N-CQDs were observed by a JEM-F200 Cold FEG TEM operated at 200 kV. Before the observation, the E-N-CQDs were dispersed in 95% (v/v) ethanol and treated with ultrasound for 30 min. One drop of the solution was then deposited onto the carbon-coated copper TEM grid (200 mesh). The E-N-CQDs were further analyzed with a Dimension Icon AFM (BRUKER, USA) in tapping mode. The surface groups were studied by XPS using an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer with a mono X-Ray source Al Kα excitation (1350.08 eV). A SEM (JSM-IT300LV, Japan) was used to observe the morphology and microstructure of CQDs. Combined with the SEM analysis, the elemental analysis was conducted using an energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS, X-MaxN20, Oxford Instruments Group, UK).

The solutions with different pH values in the range of 2.0 − 12.0 were prepared by the solutions of hydrochloric acid (10 mmol L−1), sodium citrate buffer (50 mmol L−1), phosphate buffer (50 mmol L−1), sodium bicarbonate-sodium hydroxide buffer (50 mmol L−1), and sodium hydroxide (10 mmol L−1), respectively. 100 μL E-N-CQDs solution with concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 and 1.0 mL buffer solution with different pH values were mixed uniformly. The spectra of the fluorescence intensity for these solutions were detected by the fluorescence spectrometer to find out the stability of CQDs under different pH values.

The solution with a concentration of 10 mmol L−1 for each salt (KCl, ZnCl2, CuSO4, Al(NO3)3, AgNO3, CdCl2, Ni(CH3COO)2, and Fe2(SO4)3) was first prepared using deionized water as a solvent. Then, 0.5 mL CQDs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) and 0.5 mL of various salt solutions were mixed in a 2 mL tube, respectively. The solution was then balanced for 5 min, the fluorescence spectrum was measured with the fluorescence spectrometer under the excitation wavelength of 318 nm.

Fe2(SO4)3 was dissolved in deionized water and configured to a series of solutions with various Fe(III) concentrations (0.0326, 0.0652, 0.125, 0.231, 0.321, 0.642 mmol L−1). Subsequently, 100 μL of CQDs aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1), 300 μL of Fe2(SO4)3 solution, and 1.0 mL of sodium bicarbonate-sodium hydroxide buffer solution with a pH value of 10.0 were mixed together. The solution was balanced at room temperature for 5 min, the spectrum was measured with the fluorescence spectrometer at the excitation wavelength of 318 nm.

The FTIR was used to analyze the surface chemical features of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , a very strong absorption band at 3393 cm−1 can be found, which is assigned to NH and OH from amine and hydroxyl groups stretching vibration (Rodriguez-Padron et al., 2018). There is another strong absorption band at 1607 cm-1 that can be attributed to CO stretching vibration in carboxylate and aromatic ring skeleton vibration (Miao et al., 2018; Travlou et al., 2018). Both N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs showed absorption at 1513 cm-1, which was ascribed to N-H asymmetric stretching vibration. The band at 1458 cm-1 can be linked to the symmetric vibrations of COO- groups (Kim and Sun, 2015). Meanwhile, the peaks at 1222 cm−1 and 1119 cm-1 represented the C–O stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups and C–OH, respectively. The presence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties may be due to the degradation of sugars, proteins, and lipids during the hydrothermal treatment (Cao et al., 2011). The highly functionalized surface of N-CQDs can be confirmed by these functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties (Rodriguez-Padron et al., 2018).

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–vis absorption curves of the N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs, and their photographs under UV light. The adsorption can be clearly seen at about 270 − 280 nm, which was ascribed to the p-p* transition of nanocarbon (Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013; Zhuo et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). By comparing the photographs of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs solution under UV light, as shown in Fig. 2a, it can be clearly seen that E-N-CQDs had much higher fluorescence. The maximum excitation (EX) and emission (EM) wavelengths of both N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs were nearly the same, i.e., the EX and EM were 318 nm and 414 nm, respectively, while the fluorescence intensity of E-N-CQDs was much higher than that of N-CQD at the same EX wavelength of 318 nm, as shown in Fig. 2b. 3D fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs were carried out to investigate the fluorescence performance and the corresponding emission wavelength under different excitation conditions. The results were present in Fig. 2c and d. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of E-N-CQDs is much higher than that of N-CQDs, and the best excitation spectrum has a wide range, laying a certain foundation for its application in more fields. It’s worth noting that, when the excitation wavelength increased from 200 nm to 600 nm, the emission peak of E-N-CQDs varied, the peaks of emission wavelength exhibited red shift, as shown in Fig. 2d. The results indicated that the fluorescence performance of E-N-CQDs was related to the excitation wavelength, and the luminescence process of E-N-CQDs may involve the surface defects (Chen et al., 2018). However, the fluorescence mechanism of CQDs has not been clearly investigated enough by far.
According to the fluorescence spectrum, it can be concluded that the E-N-CQDs showed good fluorescence performance. Therefore, some further analysis were conducted on E-N-CQDs in the present study.

The morphology of E-N-CQDs was investigated using TEM, HRTEM and AFM, as shown in Fig. 3 . The results showed that the average diameter of E-N-CQDs was about 9.31 nm, as shown in Fig. 3. It was clearly demonstrated from the HRTEM image that the lattice spacing was 0.25 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b, which was close to the (020) facet of graphitic carbon (Baker and Baker, 2010; Chen et al., 2018). The structure of the E-N-CQDs was further characterized by AFM. The surface and height images of E-N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. The topographical height of the E-N-CQDs was measured to be 2 − 3 nm, made of approximately 10 layers of graphite layers (Liu et al., 2018).

The XPS analysis of E-N-CQDs was further conducted, and the element contents of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) in the materials were determined and illustrated in deconvoluted curves, as shown in Fig. 4 a. From the spectra of E-N-CQDs, the bands of C1s, N1s and O1s were observed at 284.3, 399.6 and 532.5 eV, respectively (Anmei et al., 2018). C1s XPS spectrum of the raw materials EHCRs for synthesizing E-N-CQDs was obtained to have a comparison with the synthetized E-N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4b. The C1s XPS spectrum of the EHCRs showed three peaks at 284.3, 286.1, and 287.8 eV, which were attributed to CC, C–O, and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4c, the C1s spectrum of E-N-CQDs had three peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.2 eV, corresponding to different C species, i.e., (CC/CC, graphitic), C–O/CN and OCO, respectively (Rodriguez-Padron et al., 2018). The O1s spectrum of E-N-CQDs showed two peaks at 532 and 532.8 eV, which can be ascribed to CO and C–O-C, respectively (Ding et al., 2014), as shown in Fig. 4d. Moreover, the N1s spectrum of E-N-CQDs showed two main bands at 399.5 and 401.2 eV, illustrated in Fig. 4e, which was related to the presence of C-N-C and NH, respectively (Li et al., 2013). The XPS analysis further confirms the presence of NH, which is consistent with the results of FTIR, illustrating the success of passivation on the surface of resultant CQDs. In addition, the elements of C, O and N, and their distribution in E-N-CQDs were analyzed using element mapping images. Meanwhile, SEM was used to characterize the typical morphology of E-N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4f. The results showed that the elements of C (indicated by red dot) and O (indicated by pink dot) were the main components of E-N-CQDs, while the element N (indicated by yellow dot) showed less intensity and uniform distribution, suggesting that the N element was evenly distributed in E-N-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4f.

The content of elements on the surface in atomic %, and the results of the deconvolution of C1s, O1s and N1s core energy levels are summarized in Table 1 and Table 2 . The results showed that the C content in both CRs and EHCRs was quite high, indicating that these two materials can be used for the preparation of carbon dots. It was found that, compared with their corresponding raw material CRs and EHCRs, the C content in N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs increased by 6.1% and 22.1%, respectively, which would be beneficial for their fluorescence properties; the O content was decreased by 22.4% and 44.9%, respectively. In addition, the C content of E-N-CQDs was the highest, indicating that it would exhibit good fluorescence properties. In comparison with their corresponding raw materials CRs and EHCRs, the N content in N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs increased by 204.9% and 82.8%, respectively, which further confirmed that N element was successfully doped into the CQDs during hydrothermal treatment. This suggested that the surface passivation was successfully achieved, and the fluorescence properties were consequently improved.

The fluorescence intensity of E-N-CQDs at different pH values in the range of 2.0 − 12.0 was investigated and shown in Fig. 5 . The results showed that pH had an effect on the fluorescence intensity of E-N-CQDs; the E-N-CQDs exhibited strong fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 5.0 − 10.0. It is worth noting that the fluorescence reached the strongest extent level when the pH value was 10.0, which can be due to the presence of a large number of carboxyl groups on the E-N-CQDs surface (Chandra et al., 2012), and was consistent with the results of FTIR and XPS. As can be seen from Fig. 5 that E-N-CQDs had good stability, which can be attributed to the electronic transitions of π-π* and n-π* that would be changed by refilling or depleting their valance bands with the variation in pH (Aghamali et al., 2018). In other words, the synthesized E-N-CQDs were very stable at different pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of E-N-CQDs for detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the good fluorescence properties and chemical stability of E-N-CQDs, we studied their potential applications in the detection of metal ions. The fluorescence quenching effect of a variety of metal ions on N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs was determined and the results were shown in Fig. 6 a. The results showed that Ag(I), Cu(II), Fe(III) had obvious fluorescence quenching effect on both N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs, while other cations such as Zn(II), Ni(II), Cd(II), K(I) and Al(III) showed no quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of these two CQDs. E-N-CQDs displayed the most significant selectivity for Fe(III) on fluorescence quenching over other cations at a concentration of 10 mmol L−1 under otherwise the same testing conditions. The quenching effect of Fe(III) on N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs is mainly attributed to the special interactions between Fe(III) and the phenolic hydroxy groups (Zhu et al., 2013). Therefore, the presence of lignin, containing large amounts of phenolic hydroxyl groups, is responsible for the fact that E-N-CQDs are much more sensitive to Fe(III) than N-CQDs.

Fe(III) is crucial in many biological systems. Lack of Fe(III) in humans will lead to serious diseases such as anemia (Cui et al., 2018). Detection of Fe(III) in blood is of importance to monitor human health. In this part, we further assessed the sensitivity of E-N-CQDs to Fe(III) by evaluating the quenching effect. The fluorescence intensity of E-N-CQDs was measured under different concentrations of Fe(III), as shown in Fig. 6b. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching value of E-N-CQDs gradually decreased by increasing the concentration of Fe(III). The relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F0 /F) showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe(III) in the range of 0 − 321 μmol L−1, as shown in the inset of Fig. 6b.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to fit the quenching efficiency (Anmei et al., 2018):
(1)
F0 /F = 1+KSVC
where KSV is the quenching constant; C is the concentration of E-N-CQDs; F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of E-N-CQDs before and after the addition of metal ions or Fe(III) ions, respectively.

As we know, this quenching effect can be used to measure the amount of Fe(III). The detection limit of E-N-CQDs was calculated to be around 0.75 μmol L−1, which is much lower than the Fe(III)concentration in human blood (7.52 − 11.82 mmol L−1). Therefore, it is believed that the E-N-CQDs can be applied to detect the Fe(III) ions in human blood during some medical treatments.

The CRs and their enzymatically hydrolysed CRs were utilized as a new precursor to make CQDs based on a one-pot hydrothermal method. Doping N element improved the fluorescence intensity and stability of resultant CQDs, exhibiting a strong blue-green fluorescence under UV light excitation. The difference in fluorescence performance of N-CQDs and E-N-CQDs was mainly caused by the difference of the main components in raw materials, in particular, the presence of lignin-originated aromatic moieties improved the fluorescence performance of CQDs. The E-N-CQDs had the strongest fluorescence among the CODs prepared, meanwhile a good photoluminescence emission and hydrolyzation. The E-N-CQDs were the most sensitive to Fe(III) with the detection limit of 0.75 μmol L−1. The present study provides a green and sustainable approach for the synthesis of CQDs from agricultural residues, and they will have potential applications in detection of Fe(III) in blood, and other related fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 31770634 and 31300491), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City (17JCYBJC20600), and Foundation of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Pulp & Paper(No. 201802).
